title: Friendship
summary: What makes a true friend? Explore the nature of friendship, how it changes across life stages, and the qualities that make some friendships last a lifetime.

questions.text: What qualities do you think are most important in a true friend?
Do you think online friendships can be as meaningful as in-person ones?
How has social media changed the way people make and keep friends?
Is it possible to have a real friendship with someone much older or younger than you?
Describe your best friend. What makes your friendship special?
Have you ever lost a friendship? What happened and how did you feel?
Can men and women truly be 'just friends'? Why do some people doubt this?

vocabulary.definition: Showing firm and constant support or allegiance to a person.
examples: A loyal friend stands by you even when times are hard.

vocabulary.definition: Deserving of trust or confidence; dependable and reliable.
examples: She proved herself trustworthy by keeping my secret for years.

vocabulary.definition: A connection or feeling that unites two or more people.
examples: They formed a strong bond during their years of studying together.

vocabulary.definition: To gradually become less close to someone over time.
examples: We drifted apart after she moved to another city.

vocabulary.definition: A person one knows slightly but who is not a close friend.
examples: He has many acquaintances but very few real friends.

phrases: A friend in need is a friend indeed.
Quality over quantity when it comes to friendships.
True friends are hard to find.
idioms: A shoulder to cry on — someone who listens to your problems
Hit it off — to immediately become good friends
Thick as thieves — very close friends who share secrets

quotes.text: Friendship is born at the moment one person says to another, 'What! You too? I thought I was the only one.'
quotes.author: C.S. Lewis

teacher_notes: This is an excellent topic for lower-intermediate students. Use the vocabulary to practice adjective forms and comparatives. The 'can men and women be friends?' question generates lively debate.

games.name: Two Truths, One Lie — Friendship Edition
games.description: Students share three statements about their friendships. Two are true, one is a lie. The class guesses which one is false.

games.name: Friendship Spectrum
games.description: Read statements about friendship (e.g. 'Honesty is always the best policy between friends'). Students position themselves on a line from 'Agree' to 'Disagree' and explain their position.

roleplay.scenario: Your best friend forgot your birthday. How do you bring it up without causing an argument?
